coredata.dc:title: Full-colors by luminescence induced by UV excitation in carbon dots synthesized by graphite laser ablation and oxidation in liquids
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Torrisi, L.
Manno, D.
Serra, A.
Torrisi, A.
Cutroneo, M.
coredata.dc:description: A Graphite laser ablation in biocompatible phosphate buffer solution (PBS) has been produced at different laser fluences, synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence (PL). The used laser has been a repetitive ns pulsed Nd:YAG operating at 1064 nm. By exciting with a single-wavelength UV light at 365 nm, CDs emit stable luminescence in gradient colors, from blue to red. The plasma generated by the laser at the carbon-liquid interface was investigated, with the objective of giving more information on the type of CDs synthesized and on their optical properties. Optical spectroscopy was employed to analyze the CDs PL between 250 nm and 800 nm. The dispersions were submitted to UV–Vis transmittance and absorbance spectroscopies. The CDs deposition on a silicon substrate, after drying, has been analyzed with attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy. Results indicated that the emission band depends on the laser fluence and the different oxidation processes induced in the PBS solution. The quantum yield (QY) has been evaluated in the order of 15% using a neutral pH solution and about 30% using an acidic pH solution. The observed luminescence band emission shift, from about 400 nm to about 670 nm, due to a reduction of the laser fluence, has been ascribed to a gradual reduction in the CDs band gaps with the increasing incorporation of oxygen species into their surface structures. Due to the high biocompatibility, stability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent PL properties, these CDs could be used in bio-medicine and in other fields, as will be presented and discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Laser ablation
Graphite
Carbon-dots
Photoluminescence induced by UV
Optical spectroscopy
Luminescence shift

Torrisi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the experimental set-up (a) and photo of the ablation crater in the carbon-irradiated graphite (b).
TOF spectrum of C plasma ions (a) and CCD image of the expanding plasma in vacuum (b).
Photo of the perforated 1 mm graphite (a) and liquid in the beaker after 60 min laser irradiation time (b).
ATR-FTIR transmittance spectrum of the CDs dispersion obtained at 10 J/cm2 laser fluence.
FTIR transmittance spectra comparison at different laser fluences, from 50 up to 2 J/cm2.
Luminescence in CDs dispersion obtained using 50 J/cm2 fluence for 2 hr irradiation time and comparison with the background.
Luminescence in CDs dispersion using 10 J/cm2 (a) and 2 J/cm2 (b) fluence for 2 hr irradiation time.
Scheme of level deexcitation by increasing the degree of CDs surface oxidation.
Luminescence light emitted from UV excited CDs from 50 J/cm2 high laser fluence (a) up to 2 J/cm2 low laser fluence (h) with gradual fluence reduction and oxidation increasing.
CDs dispersion absorbance vs wavelength, and dispersion concentration.
TEM investigation of the CDS in PBS synthesized by the laser ablation (a), and a zoom of some CDs (b), and of one CD crystalline showing the C-atoms aggregated (c).

head.ce:title: Full-colors by luminescence induced by UV excitation in carbon dots synthesized by graphite laser ablation and oxidation in liquids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIFT Department, Physics, University of Messina, V.le F.S. D’Alcontres 31, 98166 Messina, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MIFT Department, Physics, University of Messina, V.le F.S. D’Alcontres 31, 98166 Messina, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Math. and Physics Dep.nt “E. De Giorgi”, University of Salento, V. Arnesano, 73100 Lecce, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Math. and Physics Dep.nt “E. De Giorgi”

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medicine and Surgery Dep.nt, University Enna Kore, 94100 Enna, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medicine and Surgery Dep.nt, University Enna Kore, 94100 Enna, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A Graphite laser ablation in biocompatible phosphate buffer solution (PBS) has been produced at different laser fluences, synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence (PL).
The used laser has been a repetitive ns pulsed Nd:YAG operating at 1064 nm. By exciting with a single-wavelength UV light at 365 nm, CDs emit stable luminescence in gradient colors, from blue to red.
The plasma generated by the laser at the carbon-liquid interface was investigated, with the objective of giving more information on the type of CDs synthesized and on their optical properties.
Optical spectroscopy was employed to analyze the CDs PL between 250 nm and 800 nm. The dispersions were submitted to UV–Vis transmittance and absorbance spectroscopies.
The CDs deposition on a silicon substrate, after drying, has been analyzed with attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy.
Results indicated that the emission band depends on the laser fluence and the different oxidation processes induced in the PBS solution.
The quantum yield (QY) has been evaluated in the order of 15% using a neutral pH solution and about 30% using an acidic pH solution.

The observed luminescence band emission shift, from about 400 nm to about 670 nm, due to a reduction of the laser fluence, has been ascribed to a gradual reduction in the CDs band gaps with the increasing incorporation of oxygen species into their surface structures.
Due to the high biocompatibility, stability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent PL properties, these CDs could be used in bio-medicine and in other fields, as will be presented and discussed.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Attenuated total reflectance- Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphite and other carbon target laser ablation have been investigated to generate plasma and different nanoparticles, nanostructures, thin film deposition, and carbon ion generation [1–4]. Particularly interesting is the laser ablation of graphite in liquids that permits to synthesis of nanoparticles with a large size distribution and with a significant presence of carbon dots (CDs), with a size of less than 10 nm [5,6].

CDs have many peculiar properties and, for their size comparable with the Bohr radius, have behavior like a semiconductor quantum dot. This structure generates a three-dimensional potential well that confines the charge carriers to a small region of space where the energy levels become discrete [7].
Especially if appropriately functionalized, CDs are photoluminescent (PL) in the visible region under UV excitation. Their quantum yield efficiency is generally of the order of 30 % [8].

CDs can be generated by laser ablation as a dispersion in a biocompatible liquid, to be employed for different bio-medical applications [9]. One of the liquids that can be used for biological purposes, other than the physiological solutions, is the phosphate buffer solution (PBS) generally used for cell cultures [10].

Their dispersion in PBS generates a liquid that has many applications not only for biology but also for medicine, physics, chemistry, microelectronics, sensors, solar cells, and optoelectronics [11,12,13].

Due to the high biocompatibility, stability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent PL properties, these CDs could be used in the biological field to image cells in different colors under a single wavelength light source, and for thermal- and radiotherapy, due to the high cell internalization effect of CDs with a size of the order of 1–3 nm [9,14].

The observed luminescence band emission wavelength may shift in the visible region depending on many factors, such as the size and shape of the nanoparticle, the reticule structure, the type of functionalizing molecules bonded to the CD’s surface, the CD’s oxygenation, and others. Such a factor may introduce surface states in the band gap, changing the energy band gap width and then the frequency of the emitted PL [15].
In the present experiment, the used table-top laser was a ns pulsed Nd:YAG operating at 1064 nm.

The goal of this research activity was to investigate the laser parameters to change the emission band of CDs luminescence under UV excitation, without modifying the liquid solution where the ablation occurs.

The novelty concerning other papers was to change the CDs emission luminescence in gradient colors, from blue to red, under a single-wavelength UV light exciting at 365 nm, only modifying the laser fluence.

To reach our goal, the investigation methodologies used were different optical spectroscopies, measurements of UV–Vis transmittance and absorbance, attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, and electron microscopy.

Results indicated that the emission band depends on the laser fluence, due to the different oxidation processes induced in the PBS solution. The quantum yield (QY) has been evaluated in different solution conditions.

The observed luminescence band emission shift has been ascribed to a gradual variation in the CDs band gaps with the increasing incorporation of oxygen species into their surface structures with the laser fluence, as will be presented and discussed.

The CDs applications in Biology and Medicine are particularly investigated for their possibility to generate bioimaging of healthy and diseased cells and for different ways of carrying out tissue and organ therapy using thermotherapy, drug therapy, and radiotherapy.

CDs have been generated by laser ablation in liquids at room temperature using a pulsed Nd:YAG laser operating at 1064 nm, 100 mJ pulse energy, 3 ns pulse duration, and 1 Hz repetition rate. The laser beam is focused on the target surface using a 50 cm focal lens. The laser fluence was modified by changing the laser spot on the target from 0.2 mm2 up to 8 mm2, which corresponds to a fluence range from 1.25 J/cm2 to 50 J/cm2.

The laser irradiations were performed in air and at room temperature to irradiate the target placed in the liquid, and also in a vacuum, to measure the energy of the carbon atoms emitted from the laser-generated plasma. Generally, the volume of the PBS used has been 2–4 ml, and the irradiation time is between 1 and 3 h at a 1 Hz repetition rate.

The laser properties have been measured using the time-of-flight (TOF) of the emitted ions in a vacuum, and a fast charge-coupled device (CCD) camera image of the produced plasma during 1 μs exposure time. TOF measurements use a Faraday cup with an electron suppressor to collect the ions, a flight distance of 1 m, and a fast storage oscilloscope (Tektronix TDS 5104B, 1 GHz, 5 GS/s), as reported in the literature [2].
The target was a pure graphite sample with a 2 cm x 2 cm size and 1 mm thickness by Sigma Aldrich.

The liquid where CDs are synthesized is the phosphate buffer solution (PBS) used for many biological applications. PBS by Sigma Aldrich is a solution in distilled water containing sodium chloride, sodium phosphate, and potassium chloride salts. It has a pH = 7.4 at 24 °C, and it is transparent in visible light and not luminescent under UV light [16].

The experimental apparatus consists of the laser emitting a horizontal beam with a 1 cm diameter, a 45° glass prism that deflects the laser beam vertically, a convergent lens with a 50 cm focal distance, and a glass beaker containing the carbon target immersed in the liquid. The C sample surface is placed 5 mm under the surface level of the liquid, as depicted in Fig. 1 a. The incidence angle of the laser beam is 0°. A photo of the laser ablation crater generated in the graphite target after 15 min of irradiation time is reported in Fig. 1b.

Optical spectroscopy was employed for PL, absorbance, and transmittance measurements in the near UV, visible, and near-infrared regions. For this were employed: the Avantes spectrophotometer AvaSpec-ULS204, with a wavelength range of 200–1100 nm and a resolution of 0.05–20 nm, depending on configuration, with an optical quartz fiber input; the Jasco V750 UV–Vis spectrophotometer for high-resolution measurements, operating between 200 and 900 nm with a resolution of 0.1 nm; the ATR-FTIR Jasco FT/IR 4700 spectrometer at high sensitivity operating between 400 and 4000 cm−1 wavenumber region, with 0.4 cm−1 resolution.
Optical microscopy has been applied to observe the laser-generated crater on the graphite surface.

The electron microscopy TEM has been used to observe the CD’s morphology. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded with a Jeol JEM-ARM 200F NEOARM microscope at 200 kV. Samples for TEM observations were prepared by drop-casting fresh solutions containing carbon dots onto 400 mesh copper grids coated with lacey Formvar/Carbon layers (TED PELLA, Inc. USA) and dried in natural air.
The UV lamp source (Blak-Ray lamp, model UVL-56) emits at 365 nm with a wavelength width (full width at half maximum, FWHM) of ± 10 nm. At 10 cm distance, its fluence is about 100 mJ/cm2.

The laser ablation produces plasma at the solid–liquid interface, generating an ionized vapor bubble that expands in the liquid and collapses, releasing the carbon nanoparticles, according to many literature data [17,18,19,20].

In the produced plasma, the ion collisions generate C nucleation and carbon nanostructures, which will be released into the liquid. The C ion energy was measured in vacuum through time-of-flight measurements as reported in the literature [2]. Fig. 2 a reports a typical TOF measure detecting the C ion yield vs time, at a fluence of 40 J/cm2, emitted from the graphite target from the laser shot. The spectrum shows a photopeak due to the photons emitted from the plasma and the ion peak that has a maximum yield at a carbon energy of about 1.15 keV, and a maximum kinetic energy, calculable from the temporal onset of the ion detection peak, of about 2 keV. This high energy is sufficient to produce a fast energetic ion collision with the generation of C aggregation. Fig. 2 b reports a fast CCD image of the expanding plasma in a vacuum obtained in the first 1 μs from the laser shot. The expanding velocity calculable from the plasma size is about vk = 5x103 m/s, corresponding to a kinetic energy of 1.5 keV, in agreement with the TOF datum. Assuming this velocity is attributable to the adiabatic plasma expansion in vacuum, depending on the adiabatic γ coefficient, the corresponding average temperature kT of the plasma is about:
(1) kT= (m vk 2)/γ = 1.88 eV

The ablation yield in terms of removed carbon atoms per laser pulse was evaluated by measuring the number of laser shots to perforate a graphite target 1 mm thick. Fig. 3 a reports an optical microscope photo of the hole produced in the graphite by using 50 J/cm2 fluence. The removed graphite mass was 70 μg by using an irradiation time of 1 h at a 1 Hz repetition rate, corresponding to an ablation yield Y of:
(2) Y = (Ablated mass)/(Incident laser pulse) = 19.6 ng/pulse = 9.8x1014 C atoms/pulse
a value in good agreement with the literature data [15].

Moreover, the ablation rate R has been evaluated to:
(3) R = (Removed mass)/min = 1.16 μg/min = 5.8x1016 C atoms/min

Such values decrease by about one order of magnitude using a low laser fluence of 2 J/cm2. Moreover, such values decrease with the level of liquid above the graphite surface. This is because part of the laser beam energy is absorbed by the liquid above the sample surface.

Fig. 3b shows the PBS solution in the beaker after the laser ablation of graphite for 60 min. At the bottom, there is some precipitated due to the generation of nanoparticles with micrometric size. Under white light, the solution appears transparent and not luminescent.
The precipitate mass was weighed, indicating that the removed mass is about 90 % of the total and that only about 10 % of the ablated mass produces CDs dispersed into the liquid.

The CDs dispersion was deposited on different substrates, such as Al and Si, with a smoothed and cleaned surface. Ten drops of PBS with CDs by laser ablation at different fluences, 5 μl each, were deposited on the surface and dried at 30 °C, producing a thin deposited film.

After drying, the obtained films were subjected to ATR-FTIR spectroscopy. The spectra were acquired measuring the thin film transmittance between 400 and 4000 cm−1 wavenumbers for the CDs dispersions obtained using different laser fluences, from 1 up to 50 J/cm2.

Fig. 4 reports a typical FTIR spectrum relative to the CDs dispersion obtained using a laser fluence of 10 J/cm2. Generally, the transmission is high and of the order of 95 %, except for the abundant hydroxy group O–H present between 3600 and 2700 cm−1.
The CDs dispersion contains different functional oxygen groups, such as carboxylic (C=O, 1650 cm−1), carbonylic (COOH, 1759 cm−1), hydroxyl (O–H, 3425 cm−1), water, and others.

The spectrum shows that the CD samples contain high hydroxy groups, O–H, and carboxylic groups. Maybe that there are present also nitrene groups N–H (3200 cm−1) and carbonyl groups C-O (1258 cm−1) in lower concentrations. Moreover, the spectrum shows the C=N stretching vibrations (1642 cm−1), the presence of the CO2 groups, which can be convolved with the alkyne groups C≡C (2350 cm−1), and C=C groups (750 cm−1), and the groups =CH2 (850 cm−1).

Such peaks are present in the different samples of CD dispersions realized at different laser fluences, but their concentration is different because it decreases at high laser fluences and increases at low fluences.

Fig. 5 shows an FTIR spectra comparison obtained by analyzing the CD dispersions at the fluence of 50 J/cm2, 20 J/cm2, 10 J/cm2, and 2 J/cm2. It appears that the total transmission increases increasing the laser fluence used for the CDs synthesis.

Spectra indicate that CDs contain a very large contribution from residual water (a broad peak at 3300 cm−1 and a peak at 1650 cm−1), and no actual variations in chemical composition between samples can be traced. The signal on the FTIR spectra near 2300 cm−1 is due to CO2 absorption in the air rather than from CDs.

The increase of the oxygen functional groups in the CDs suspension, especially of the OH hydroxy groups, with the laser fluence decreasing, indicates that the CDs are more oxidized at low fluence than the high fluence. This effect may be due to the different surfaces irradiated by the laser beam using different spots and fluencies. When the spot is large, i.e., at low fluence, a major area of graphite is exposed to the ablation beam, and the plasma produced at the solid–liquid interface contains more oxygen. This effect causes major CDs oxidation, i.e., major functional oxygen groups bonded to the carbon nanoparticle surfaces generated in the liquid. This effect of the oxidation indicates that the CDs have a functionalization depending on their oxygen content, which can be controlled by the laser spot size. Further analyses concerning the level of CDs oxidation by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) are in progress.

According to the literature, the carboxyl groups on sp2-hybridized carbons could induce significant local distortions with the formation of surface energy level states, resulting in a narrower energy gap [21]. A higher degree of oxidation on the CD surfaces implies the presence of more surface defects [22].

The CDs dispersion presents a high photoluminescence in the visible region under excitation by UV laser light at 365 nm. This luminescence is proportional to the CDs concentration in the dispersion, i.e., to the laser irradiation time employed to synthesize the CDs from graphite ablation, as demonstrated in our previous paper [6]. Fig. 6 reports a luminescence spectrum obtained at high laser fluence, 50 J/cm2, producing a blue light emission, obtained after 2-hour laser graphite irradiation at 1 Hz in 4 ml PBS solution, and the comparison with the background spectrum obtained irradiating only PBS solution without CDs.

The photoluminescence spectrum induced by the UV lamp presents a main band centered at 475 nm (blue region) and a secondary band, less intense, centered at about 510 nm (green). From an approximated deconvolution with a Gaussian shape, the first band peak has a width (FWHM) of about 80 nm and the second of about 100 nm, respectively.

Modifying the laser ablation fluence from high to low values, the photoluminescence spectrum shows a shift towards the red region. Fig. 7 a reports a typical spectrum obtained by CDs in PBS synthesized using a laser fluence of 20 J/cm2, while Fig. 7b reports a fluence of 2 J/cm2. In the two cases, the laser irradiation time was 120 min, and in the first case, the luminescence was in the green-yellow band, while in the second case in the orange-red band.

In the case of 10 J/cm2 fluence, the luminescent band is centered at 565 nm and presents two lateral bands, one at about 480 nm and the other at about 670 nm. The central band is the main one and has a width of about 150 nm. The CD’s dispersion emits a green-yellow light color. In the case of the lower 2 J/cm2 fluence, the luminescence has a main peak at 660 nm, but the yield shows a continuum growth from 450 nm up to 660 nm, indicating that the luminescence has contributions also at lower wavelengths. The CD’s dispersion emits an orange-red light color. Probably the size of the CDs and the oxidation states have a large distribution, determining this type of spectrum, presenting the contribution of luminescence at different wavelengths. The visible PL emission of each sample originated from the UV absorption in the higher photon energy region.

The results reported in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 concerning the CDs luminescence spectra shift towards the higher wavelength by reducing the laser fluence represent a novelty of this paper concerning the literature data. In particular, decreasing the fluence, by enhancing the laser spot areas on the graphite in the liquid, the laser splash effect in the liquid enhances, and the larger laser spot area increases the oxidation process of CDs surface, modifying their luminescence spectra emission under UV excitation, shifting it toward the red region.

Literature reports that the possible explanations more adapted to justify the luminescence shift toward the red with the laser fluence are due to the band gap transitions in conjugated π-domains, and to surface defects and functionalized molecules on the CDs [14,23]. The structural characterizations obtained from FTIR spectroscopy confirmed that both the carboxyl contents and the oxidation degrees of the CD surfaces increased with the redshift of the PL due to the lower laser fluence. A higher degree of oxidation on the CD surfaces implies more surface defects and, at the same time, the carboxyl groups on sp2-hybridized carbons could induce significant local distortions, resulting in a narrower energy gap. Thus, the analyzed luminescence redshift arises because of the increase in the degree of surface oxidation due to large laser spots or low laser fluence. The excitation occurs via UV light, and the deexcitation occurs through increasingly closer energy levels as oxidation increases.

Fig. 8 shows a scheme of the possible transitions occurring for the deexcitation from absorbed UV photons as the gap is reduced by the intermediate energy levels introduced by the oxidation processes of the CD surface.
Fig. 9 reports some photos of a series of measurements of UV-induced luminescence of CDs in PBS obtained by irradiating graphite at different laser spots, i.e., at different fluences.

The photos indicate the luminescence light emission from the very blue (a), obtained at the high fluence of 50 J/cm2, to the less blue emission (b, c), then green (d), yellow (e, f) at 10 J/cm2, orange (g) and finally red (h) at 2 J/cm2 fluence.

The luminescence is not produced in pure distilled water containing CDs by graphite laser ablation and under UV illumination, indicating that it is not due to pure CDs. Moreover, the luminescence is not produced in only PBS solution without CDs under UV illumination, indicating that it is not due to the salts used in the PBS solution. Such results were published previously, as reported in the ref. [10]. Thus, the luminescence induced by UV photons is not only due to CD’s surface oxidation but also to CDs functionalization by the salts present in the PBS solution, which contain sodium, chlorine, phosphorus, and potassium and whose molecules functionalize the surfaces of CDs.
As reported in our previous article, the luminescence yield increases with the laser irradiation time, i.e., with the concentration of CDs in the PBS solution [6].

By using the UV lamp fluence at a 10 cm distance, the cuvette size and its solid angle of UV irradiation, and measuring the number of emitted luminescence photons through the 300 μm diameter fiber connected to the optical spectrometer Avantes, it was possible to evaluate the quantum yield (QY) value. QY represents the number of luminescent emitted photons concerning the incident one.

The number of exciting photons into the CDs dispersion was measured by the photons emitted in 30 s from the UV lamp through the quartz fiber using the optical spectrometer recording. The number of photons emitted for luminescence was measured through the solid angle of a fiber detecting the CDs luminescence and extending the emission to 4π str, assuming an isotropic luminescence emission.

The experimental value obtained in CDs suspension synthesized at a high laser fluence of 50 J/cm2 for an irradiation time of 200 min was:
(4) QY = (Emitted luminescence (475 ± 80 nm))/(Incident photons (365 ± 10 nm)) ≈ 15 %

This value, referred to as blue luminescence, is comparable but slightly lower than those reported in literature, around 30 % [24]. The lower quantum yield could be due to the many other parameters that influence it, such as the pH of the solution, the ions produced in the liquid, the temperature, the salts of the PBS solution, and others. Furthermore, it must be considered that the current measurements presented have not yet been optimized to achieve the maximum QY values achievable.
In our previous article, measurements performed using an acid PBS solution, with a pH = 3, have demonstrated at high dose, for blue luminescence, an increment of QY value up to about 30 % [25].
The CDs dispersion absorbance was measured through UV–Vis spectroscopy. A typical result is reported in the spectra of Fig. 10 .

The absorbance is high in the UV region and decreases for higher wavelengths, depending on the level of concentration of the CDs in the solution. The discontinuities in the absorbance decay with the wavelength are present in the UV region, indicating that small nanostructures are present in the liquid dispersion, as reported in the literature for nanoparticle sizes lower than 10 nm [26,27].

The absorbance of CDs in the UV region represents peculiar characteristics of these nanoparticles with respect to their quantum effects and structural heterogeneity. Their very high absorption spectrum is mainly located in the near ultraviolet region, attributable to π-π* transitions. In the 280–350 nm region, the absorbance effect is attributable to n-π* transitions involving C–C or C=O bonds, reflecting the structural diversity and compositional variability of CDs. Outside this range, the absorption intensity decreases between the visible and the near infrared (NIR).

Further investigations were performed using TEM microscopy to measure the CD’s size and shape. Fig. 11 a reports a photo of a sample prepared to observe the CDs synthesized in PBS, which indicates an average size of about 2–3 nm. More accurately, the shape is ellipsoidal with a major axis of 2.7 nm and a lower of 1.7 nm, as calculable from Fig. 11b. Special attention is given to the zoom of such very small nanoparticles, reported in Fig. 11c, which indicates a series of ordered atoms, i.e., a crystalline structure of carbon clusters. These results therefore indicate that the high energy transferred from the plasma to the carbon atoms can induce their nucleation and crystallization. Further analyses are in progress on this peculiar aspect of the CD’s atomic structure and its lattice period in comparison with the original graphite.
With the above-cited dimensions, the single CD has a volume of about 32.7 nm3 and, assuming a graphite density of 2.25 g/cm3, its mass is about 7.36x10-20 g, corresponding to 3680C aggregated atoms.

In this paper, we have demonstrated that the CDs synthesis may occur by ablating graphite in a PBS biocompatible liquid. The proposed method is simple, low cost, reliable, and reproducible, very biocompatible because it does not use toxic materials or liquids that can be contaminants of the solution used, and allows to obtaining of luminescence of different colors.

The used laser was an Nd:YAG Q-switched at 3 ns, operating in the fundamental frequency at 1064 nm with 100 mJ pulse energy and 1 Hz repetition rate. The spot was changed to control the fluence between 1 and 50 J/cm2. The maximum ablation yield was about 19.6 ng/pulse, and the ablation rate of about 1.16 μg/min.

The CDs luminescence excited by UV photons at 365 nm falls into the visible region. The luminescence grows linearly with the laser ablation time, i.e., with the concentration of CDs in the dispersion. It depends on the used laser fluence, showing a red shift with the decrease in the laser fluence, i.e., with the increase of the laser spot on the graphite surface, maintaining a constant laser pulse energy of 100 mJ. At 50 J/cm2 high fluence, the luminescence is blue around 475 nm, while at the lower fluence of 2 J/cm2, it is red, around 660 nm.

The red shift of the luminescence with the laser fluence is explained as mainly dependent on the CDs oxidation, as was evident from the FTIR analyses. However, other parameters may influence the luminescent band, due to the different salts present in the PBS solution, the size distribution of the obtained CDs, the different ions present in the solution, the temperature, and other parameters.

CDs possessed stable and bright luminescence in PBS with a neutral pH value, but this can be increased using an acidic pH value. The quantum luminescence was evaluated of the order of 15 % but it can be increased by optimizing the parameters concerning the laser (wavelength, pulse width and energy, pulse duration, irradiation time,…) the used solution (PBS concentration, solution pH value, additive to the solution,…) and the irradiation conditions (temperature, depth of the graphite concerning the free liquid surface, laser spot size,…).

The FTIR measurements demonstrate that the different surface states increase with the fluence decreasing, leading to a narrowing of the gap due to intermediate surface states between the fluence and conduction bands, caused by oxidation processes at low fluences, i.e., under conditions of large ablated surfaces. Thus, full-color emission from CDs, due to the redshift by laser fluence, can be obtained depending on their level of oxidation.

Furthermore, TEM investigation demonstrated that the CDs structure is constituted by ordered atoms as a carbon crystal, a resistant spheroidal nanoparticle that ensures stability and surface properties that favor its diffusivity and mobility.

The results obtained find many applications in different fields, from optics to solar cells, from biology to medicine, and from bioimaging in different materials and in biological tissues and cells, as reported in many literature [28,29]. Special applications are also found in bio-medicine for diagnostics and therapy [9,30].

L. Torrisi: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition. D. Manno: Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. A. Serra: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. A. Torrisi: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition. M. Cutroneo: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the Ministero dell'Università e della Ricerca for the Italian PRIN-PNRR 'Carbon dots for advanced bio-medical imaging and beyond (CAR-BIO-MED)’ CUP J53D23016220001-CI P2022M33MS coordinated at MIFT Department of Messina University by Prof. L. Torrisi.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Laser ablation of graphite with near infrared microsecond pulses

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Doualle, M. Reymond, Y. Pontillon, L. Gallais, Laser ablation of graphite with near infrared microsecond pulses. Appl. Phys. A 127 (2021) 722.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: IR pulsed laser ablation of carbon materials in high vacuum
